elements.sent-2411-001: We contribute to the theory of (homotopy) colimits inside homotopy type theory.
elements.sent-2411-002: The heart of our work characterizes the connection between colimits in coslices of a universe, called coslice colimits, and colimits in the universe (i.e., ordinary colimits).
elements.sent-2411-003: To derive this characterization, we find an explicit construction of colimits in coslices that is tailored to reveal the connection.
elements.sent-2411-004: We use the construction to derive properties of colimits.
elements.sent-2411-005: Notably, we prove that the forgetful functor from a coslice creates colimits over trees.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also use the construction to examine how colimits interact with orthogonal factorization systems and with cohomology theories.
elements.sent-2411-007: As a consequence of their interaction with orthogonal factorization systems, all pointed colimits (special kinds of coslice colimits) preserve $n$-connectedness, which implies that higher groups are closed under colimits on directed graphs.
elements.sent-2411-008: We have formalized our main construction of the coslice colimit functor in Agda.
elements.sent-2411-009: The code for this paper is available at \url{https://github.com/PHart3/colimits-agda .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Working in homotopy type theory (HoTT), we study higher inductive types (HITs) arising as (homotopy) colimits in coslices of a universe, called coslice colimits.
elements.sent-2411-011: Coslices of a universe are type-theoretic versions of coslice categories.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our study of coslice colimits is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-013: We want to construct all colimits in $A/\U$, or $A$-colimits.
elements.sent-2411-014: The (wild) category $A/\U$ has objects $\sum_{T : \U A \to T$ with $X \to_A Y \coloneqq \sum_{k : \pr_1(X) \to \pr_1(Y) k \circ \pr_2(X) \sim \pr_2(Y)$ as morphisms from $X$ to $Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: HoTT has a general schema for HITs that would let us simply postulate $A$-colimits.
elements.sent-2411-016: We, however, explicitly construct $A$-colimits with just the machinery of Martin-L\"of type theory (MLTT) augmented with pushouts.
elements.sent-2411-017: We take this different approach to reveal the connection between $A$-colimits and their underlying colimits in $\U$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Therefore, we call the construction of \cref{Constr the main connection.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In fact, our construction is not a case of a general method to encode higher-dimensional HITs with pushouts but rather tailored to reveal this connection.
elements.sent-2411-020: This connection sheds light on other areas of synthetic homotopy theory, which we discuss now.
elements.sent-2411-021: The main connection will establish a well-known classical result inside type theory: The forgetful functor $A/\U \to \U$ creates colimits of diagrams over contractible graphs (\cref{create ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: We review such graphs, known as trees, in \cref{trees .}
elements.sent-2411-023: With the forgetful functor creating colimits, we can transfer universality of ordinary colimits to $A$-colimits in many cases (\cref{univcolim ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: This is notable as LCC $\infty$-categories are not closed under coslices.
elements.subsection-2411-003: The categories of higher groups are cocomplete (\cref{Presv}
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cref{Presv , we use the main connection to show that colimits in $A/\U$ preserve left classes of maps of such systems on $\U$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: It is significant that we consider systems on $\U$ rather than $A/\U$.
elements.sent-2411-027: We could derive a similar preservation theorem for systems on $A/\U$ directly from the universal property of an $A$-colimit.
elements.sent-2411-028: In practice, however, the factorization systems we tend to care about are on $\U$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Since the main connection relates the action of $A$-colimits on maps to the action of their underlying colimits on maps, we manage to deduce the preservation theorem for systems on $\U$.
elements.sent-2411-030: To prove this theorem, we find it useful to develop the theory of factorization systems in a more general setting than $\U$.
elements.sent-2411-031: In \cref{OFS , we study such systems on wild categories, which make up one approach to category theory in HoTT.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We prove that if a functor $F$ of well-behaved wild categories with factorization systems has a right adjoint $G$, then $F$ preserves the left class when $G$ preserves the right class (\cref{fspres ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: We combine this result with the main connection to deduce the desired preservation property.
elements.sent-2411-034: When we focus on the ($n$-connected, $n$-truncated) system on $\U$~\cite[Chapter 7.6]{Uni13 and take $A$ as the unit type, the main connection shows that the colimit of every diagram of pointed $n$-connected types is $n$-connected.}
elements.sent-2411-035: One useful corollary of this is that the higher category ${\left(n,k\right)\mathsf{GType }$ of $k$-tuply groupal $n$-groupoids considered by~\cite{Higher is cocomplete on (directed) graphs for all truncation levels ${-2 \leq n \leq \infty$ and ${-1 \leq k < \infty$ (\cref{cocomp ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We also exploit the generality of the main connection to extend this cocompleteness result to categories of higher pointed abelian groups (\cref{pthicol ).}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Cohomology sends colimits to weak limits (\cref{contcohom}
elements.sent-2411-037: To do so, we consider weak limits, which are key ingredients in the Brown representability theorem.
elements.sent-2411-038: A weak colimit in a category need not satify the uniqueness property required of a colimit.
elements.sent-2411-039: The Brown representability theorem specifies conditions for a presheaf on the homotopy category $\mathbf{Ho (\mathbf{Top _{\ast,c )$ of pointed connected spaces to be representable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The known proof of this theorem requires the presheaf to send countable homotopy colimits in $\mathbf{Top _{\ast,c}$ to weak limits in $\mathbf{Set}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Eilenberg-Steenrod cohomology theories enjoy this property as set-valued functors.
elements.sent-2411-042: In \cref{Contr2 , we use the main connection to establish a restricted, type-theoretic version of this property.}
elements.sent-2411-043: From the main connection we derive another construction of $A$-colimits, as pushouts of coproducts (\cref{coeqcop ), which mirrors a well-known classical lemma.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We take $A$ as the unit type and combine the new construction with the Mayer-Vietoris sequence to find that cohomology takes finite colimits to weak limits assuming the axiom of choice.

elements.sent-2411-048: We assume the reader is familiar with MLTT and HITs in the style of~\cite{Uni13 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: We will work in MLTT augmented with ordinary colimits and denote this system by $\mathsf{MLTT + Colim}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In particular, the entirety of \cref{Constr takes place inside $\mathsf{MLTT + Colim}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In fact, we need only augment MLTT with pushouts as they let us construct all nonrecursive $1$-HITs, including ordinary colimits, with all of their computational properties.
elements.sent-2411-052: Notably, $\mathsf{MLTT + Colim}$ comes with strong function extensionality for free.
elements.sent-2411-053: This property is critical for reasoning about functions in type theory and underlies almost all our work.

elements.sent-2411-201: Note that $\colimm_{\Gamma}$ is a functor from the wild category of diagrams over $\Gamma$ to $\U$.
elements.sent-2411-202: In particular, for each $\left(\alpha, p\right) : F \Rightarrow G$, the function $\colimm(\alpha, p) : \colimm_{\Gamma (F) \to \colimm_{\Gamma (G)$ is the canonical map induced by the following cocone under $F$: \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[ampersand replacement=\&, row sep = 12] {F_i} \&\& {F_j} {G_i} \& {} \& {G_j} \& {\colimm_{\Gamma}(G)} \arrow["{\alpha_i}"', from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["{\alpha_j}", from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{F_{i,j,g}}", from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{\iota_i}"', from=2-1, to=3-2] \arrow["{\iota_j}", from=2-3, to=3-2] \arrow["{G_{i,j,g}}", dotted, from=2-1, to=2-3] \end{tikzcd} \tag{$\lambda{x}.\ap_{\iota_j}(p_{i,j,g}(x))^{-1} \cdot \kappa^G_{i,j,g}(\alpha_i(x))$}\end{equation} Moreover, the pushout HIT is a functor on spans.}}

elements.sent-2411-205: An $A$-cocone $\left(C, r, p\right)$ under $F$ is colimiting if \begin{equation}\postcomp(C,r,p,T) \ : \ \left(C \to_A T\right) \to Cocone_F(T) \postcomp(C,r,p,T)(f, f_p) \ \coloneqq \ \qquad .8{$\left(\lambda{i}. \left(f \circ \pr_1(r_i), \lambda{a}.\ap_f(\pr_2(r_i)(a)) \cdot f_p(a)\right), \lambda{i}\lambda{j}\lambda{g}.\left(\lambda{x}.\ap_f(\pr_1(p_{i,j,g})(x)), \lambda{a}.\Theta_{\pr_1(p_{i,j,g})}(f^{\ast},a) \cdot \ap_{{-} \cdot f_p(a)}(\ap_{\ap_f}(\pr_2(p_{i,j,g})(a))) \right) \right)$}\end{equation} is an equivalence for every $T : A/\U$, where $\Theta_{\pr_1(p_{i,j,g ) (f^{\ast ,a) $ has type \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[row sep = 10] {\ap_f(\pr_1(p_{i,j,g})(\pr_2(F_i)(a)))^{-1} \cdot \ap_{f \circ \pr_1(r_j)}(\pr_2(F_{i,j,g})(a)) \cdot \ap_f(\pr_2(r_j)(a)) \cdot f_p(a)} {\ap_f(\pr_1(p_{i,j,g})(\pr_2(F_i)(a))^{-1} \cdot \ap_{\pr_1(r_j)}(\pr_2(F_{i,j,g})(a)) \cdot \pr_2(r_j)(a)) \cdot f_p(a)} \arrow[Rightarrow, no head, from=1-1, to=2-1] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} and is defined by double path induction on $\pr_1(p_{i,j,g )(\pr_2(F_i)(a))$ and $\pr_2(F_{i,j,g )(a)$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-211: For all $i, j : \Gamma_0$ and $g : \Gamma_1(i,j)$, the commuting triangle of an $A$-cocone \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd} {F_i} {F_j} C \arrow["{F_{i,j,g}}", from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{r_i}"', from=1-1, to=2-2] \arrow["{r_j}", from=1-3, to=2-2] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} is equivalent to a homotopy $\eta_{i,j,g : \pr_1(r_j) \circ \pr_1(F_{i,j,g ) \sim \pr_1(r_i)$ equipped with a commuting square \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[row sep = large, column sep = 35] {\pr_1(r_j)(\pr_1(F_{i,j,g})(\pr_2(F_i)(a)))} {\pr_1(r_i)(\pr_2(F_i)(a))} {\pr_1(r_j)(\pr_2(F_j)(a))} {\pr_2(Y)(a)} \arrow["{\eta_{i,j,g}(\pr_2(F_i)(a))}", Rightarrow, from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{\ap_{\pr_1(r_j)}(\pr_2(F_{i,j,g})(a))}"', Rightarrow, from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["{\pr_2(r_i)(a)}", Rightarrow, from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{\pr_2(r_j)(a) }"', Rightarrow, from=2-1, to=2-3] \end{tikzcd} \label{etaeq} \tag{\end{equation}2$-c$}$$ of paths for each $a :A$.}}

elements.sent-2411-219: From this data, we can construct a diagram $\zeta(\Delta, G)$ over a graph $\Gamma$: \begin{equation} \Gamma_0 \ \coloneqq \ \Delta_0 + \1 \Gamma_1(\inl(i), \inl(j)) \ \coloneqq \ \Delta_1(i,j) \Gamma_1(\inl(i), \inr(\ast)) \ \coloneqq \ \0 \Gamma_1(\inr(\ast), \inl(i)) \ \coloneqq \ \1 \Gamma_1(\inr(\ast), \inr(\ast)) \ \coloneqq \ \0   \zeta(\Delta, G)_{\inl(i)} \ \coloneqq \ \pr_1(G_i) \zeta(\Delta, G)_{\inr(\ast)} \ \coloneqq \ A \zeta(\Delta, G)_{\inl(i), \inl(j), g} \ \coloneqq \ \pr_1(G_{i,j,g}) \zeta(\Delta, G)_{\inr(\ast), \inl(i), \ast} \ \coloneqq \ \pr_2(G_i) \end{equation} If $\Delta$ is discrete, then the pushout \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[column sep = 80, row sep = large] {\Delta_0 \times A} {\sum_{i : \Delta_0}\pr_1(G_i)} A D \arrow["{\left(i, a\right) \mapsto \left(i, \pr_2(G_i)(a)\right)}", from=1-1, to=1-2] \arrow["{\pr_2}"', from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["\inr", from=1-2, to=2-2] \arrow["\inl"', from=2-1, to=2-2] \arrow["\lrcorner"{anchor=center, pos=0.125, rotate=180}, draw=none, from=2-2, to=1-1] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} together with $\inl$ is the coproduct of the $G_i$ in $A/\U$.

elements.sent-2411-223: Define \begin{equation}\left(\varphi, \alpha\right) \ : \ \left(\bigvee_{x : \Delta}G_x\right) \to \colimm_{x : \Gamma}\zeta(\Delta, G,x) \varphi(\inl(a)) \ \coloneqq \ \iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a) \varphi(\inr(i, x)) \ \coloneqq \ \iota_{\inl(i)}(x) \ap_{\varphi}(\glue(a,i)) \ = \ \kappa_{\zeta(\Delta,G)}(\inr(\ast), \inl(i),a )^{-1} \cdot \ap_{\iota_{\inl(i)}}(\pr_2(G_{\inr(\ast), \inl(i), \ast})(a)) \alpha \ \coloneqq \ \lambda{a}.\refl_{\iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a)}\end{equation} Conversely, define \begin{equation}\psi \ : \ \left(\colimm_{x : \Gamma}\zeta(\Delta, G,x) \right) \to \bigvee_{x : \Delta}G_x \psi(\iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a)) \ \coloneqq \ \inl(a) \psi(\iota_{\inl(i)}(x)) \ \coloneqq \ \inr(i,x) \ap_{\psi}(\kappa_{\zeta(\Delta,G)}(\inr(\ast), \inl(i),a)) \ = \ \ap_{\inr(i, {-})}(\pr_2(G_{\inr(\ast), \inl(i), \ast})(a)) \cdot \glue(a, i)^{-1}\end{equation} It is easy to prove that $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are mutual inverses as ordinary functions.

elements.sent-2411-276: Pointed acyclic types are closed under $\colimm_{\Gamma ^{\ast}$.\footnote{Recall that a type is acyclic if its suspension is contractible (see \cite{acyc ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-277: Since $\Sigma : \U^{\ast \to \U^{\ast}$ is a $2$-coherent left adjoint (\cref{suspcol ), we have that $\Sigma(\colimm_{\Gamma ^{\ast (F)) \simeq \colimm_{i : \Gamma ^{\ast (\Sigma(F_i))$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-493: Consider terms \begin{equation}D \ : \ \prod_{x :A}B(x) \to C(x) \to \U \qquad \quad d \ : \ D(a,b,c)\end{equation} If $\sum_{y : B(a) D(a,y,c)$ is contractible, then the type family \begin{equation}\left(x,y\right) \ \mapsto \ \sum_{z : C(x)}D(x,y,z)\end{equation} is an identity system on $\left(\sum_{x:A B(x), \left(a,b\right)\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-494: We prove that $2$-coherent left adjoints between wild categories preserve colimits over graphs.
elements.sent-2411-495: Suppose that $L : \C \to \D$ is a functor of wild categories and that $\left(\alpha, V_1, V_2\right) : L \dashv R$ (see \cref{adjdef ).}
elements.sent-2411-496: We say that $L$ is 2-coherent if for all $h_1 : \homm_{\D (L(X), Y)$, $h_2 : \homm_{\C (Z, X)$, and $h_3 : \homm_{\C (W, Z)$, the diagram \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[column sep = huge] {\left( \alpha(h_1) \circ h_2\right) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1) \circ \left(h_2 \circ h_3\right)} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2)) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2 \circ h_3))} {\alpha(\left(h_1 \circ L(h_2)\right) \circ L(h_3))} {\alpha(h_1 \circ \left(L(h_2) \circ L(h_3) \right))} \arrow["{{\assoc(\alpha(h_1), h_2, h_3)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_2 \circ h_3, h_1)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{\ap_{{-} \circ h_3}(V_2(h_2,h_1))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-1, to=1-1] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\ap_{h_1 \circ {-}}(L_2(h_2,h_3)))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-3, to=3-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_3,h_1 \circ L(h_2))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=2-1] \arrow[dashed, no head, from=3-1, to=2-3] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\assoc(h_1, L(h_2), L(h_3)))}}"', Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=3-3] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} commutes, i.e., the string of paths above the dashed line equals the string of paths below it.}}}
elements.formula-2411-239: \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[column sep = huge] {\left( \alpha(h_1) \circ h_2\right) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1) \circ \left(h_2 \circ h_3\right)} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2)) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2 \circ h_3))} {\alpha(\left(h_1 \circ L(h_2)\right) \circ L(h_3))} {\alpha(h_1 \circ \left(L(h_2) \circ L(h_3) \right))} \arrow["{{\assoc(\alpha(h_1), h_2, h_3)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_2 \circ h_3, h_1)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{\ap_{{-} \circ h_3}(V_2(h_2,h_1))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-1, to=1-1] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\ap_{h_1 \circ {-}}(L_2(h_2,h_3)))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-3, to=3-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_3,h_1 \circ L(h_2))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=2-1] \arrow[dashed, no head, from=3-1, to=2-3] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\assoc(h_1, L(h_2), L(h_3)))}}"', Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=3-3] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-497: Let $\C$ be a wild category and $\Gamma$ be a graph.
elements.sent-2411-499: We say that a cocone $\left(C, r, K\right)$ under $F$ is colimiting if for all $X : \obb(\C)$, \begin{equation}\postcomp(C,r, K,X) \ : \ \homm_{\C}(C, X) \to \limm_{i : \Gamma^{\op}}(\homm_{\C}(F_i, X)) \postcomp(C,r,K,X,f) \ \coloneqq \ \left(\lambda{i}.f \circ r_i, \lambda{j}\lambda{i}\lambda{g}.\assoc(f, r_j, F_{i,j,g}) \cdot \ap_{f \circ {-}}(K_{i,j,g}) \right)\end{equation} is an equivalence.
elements.formula-2411-240: \begin{equation}\postcomp(C,r, K,X) \ : \ \homm_{\C}(C, X) \to \limm_{i : \Gamma^{\op}}(\homm_{\C}(F_i, X)) \postcomp(C,r,K,X,f) \ \coloneqq \ \left(\lambda{i}.f \circ r_i, \lambda{j}\lambda{i}\lambda{g}.\assoc(f, r_j, F_{i,j,g}) \cdot \ap_{f \circ {-}}(K_{i,j,g}) \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-501: Suppose that $L : \C \to \D$ and $R : \D \to \C$ are functors of wild categories and that $\left(\alpha, V_1, V_2\right) : L\dashv R$.
elements.sent-2411-504: We have an induced cocone \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[row sep = large] {L_0(F_i)} {L_0(F_j)} {L_0(C)} \arrow[""{name=0, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "{L_1(F_{i,j,g})}", from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{L_1(r_i)}"', from=1-1, to=2-2] \arrow["{L_1(r_j)}", from=1-3, to=2-2] \arrow["{L(K_{i,j,g})}"{description}, draw=none, from=0, to=2-2] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} under $L(F)$.

elements.sent-2411-512: An adjunction $L \dashv R$ consists of terms \begin{equation}\alpha \ : \ \prod_{A : \obb(\C)}\prod_{ X : \obb(\D)}\homm_{\D}(L{A}, X) \simeq \homm_{\C}(A,R{X}) V_1 \ : \ \prod_{A :\obb(\C)}\prod_{ X, Y : \obb(\D)}\prod_{g : \homm_{\D}(X,Y)}\prod_{h : \homm_{\D}(L{A}, X)}R{g} \circ \alpha(h) = \alpha(g \circ h) V_2 \ : \ \prod_{Y : \obb(\D)}\prod_{ A, B : \obb(\C)}\prod_{f : \homm_{\C}(A,B)}\prod_{h : \homm_{\D}(L{B}, Y)}\alpha(h) \circ f = \alpha(h \circ L{f}).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-549: A morphism $f : \homm_{\C (A,B)$ of $\C$ is an (internal) equivalence if it is biinvertible: \begin{equation}\isequiv(f) \ \coloneqq \ \sum_{g,h : \homm_{\C}(B,A)}g \circ f = \idd_A \times f \circ h = \idd_B\end{equation} We write $A \simeq_{\C B$ for the type of equivalences from $A$ to $B$.}}
elements.sent-2411-550: We say that $\C$ is univalent if for all $A, B : \obb(\C)$, the following function is an equivalence: \begin{equation}idtoequiv \ : \ \mleft(A =_{\obb(\C)} B\mright) \to \mleft(A \simeq_{\C} B\mright) \refl_A \ \mapsto \ \mleft(\idd_A, \idd_A, \idd_A, LId(\idd_A), LId(\idd_A)\mright)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-551: A functor $F : \C \to \D$ from $\C$ to $\D$ is a tuple consisting of \begin{equation}F_0 \ : \ \obb(\C) \to \obb(\D) F_1 \ : \ \prod_{X,Y : \obb(\C)}\homm_{\C}(X,Y) \to \homm_{\D}(F_0(X), F_0(Y)) F_{\circ} \ : \ \prod_{X,Y,Z : \obb(\C)}\prod_{g : \homm_{\C}(Y,Z)}\prod_{f : \homm_{\C}(X,Y)} F_1(g \circ f) = F_1(g) \circ F_1(f) F_{\idd} \ : \ \prod_{X : \obb(\C)}F_1(\idd_X) = \idd_{F_0(X)}\end{equation} We may refer to $F_0$ or $F_1$ by $F$.
elements.formula-2411-263: \begin{equation}F_0 \ : \ \obb(\C) \to \obb(\D) F_1 \ : \ \prod_{X,Y : \obb(\C)}\homm_{\C}(X,Y) \to \homm_{\D}(F_0(X), F_0(Y)) F_{\circ} \ : \ \prod_{X,Y,Z : \obb(\C)}\prod_{g : \homm_{\C}(Y,Z)}\prod_{f : \homm_{\C}(X,Y)} F_1(g \circ f) = F_1(g) \circ F_1(f) F_{\idd} \ : \ \prod_{X : \obb(\C)}F_1(\idd_X) = \idd_{F_0(X)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-552: If the data $F_{\circ}$ and $F_{\idd}$ are omitted, then we call $F$ a $0$-functor.
elements.sent-2411-554: A natural transformation $\tau : F \to G$ from $F$ to $G$ consists of \begin{equation}\tau_0 \ : \ \prod_{X : \obb(\C)}\homm_{\D}(F(X), G(X)) \tau_1 \ : \ \prod_{X, Y : \obb(\C)}\prod_{f : \homm_{\C}(X , Y)}G(f) \circ \tau_0(X) = \tau_0(Y) \circ F(f)\end{equation} We say that $\tau$ is a (natural) isomorphism if $\tau_0(X)$ is an equivalence for each $X : \obb(C)$.

elements.sent-2411-578: We have the commuting diagram \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[row sep = large, column sep = large] A {\im(f)} X B {\im(g)} Y \arrow["{{{s_f}}}"{description}, from=1-1, to=1-2] \arrow[""{name=0, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "f", curve={height=-30pt}, from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["l"', from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["{{{t_f}}}"{description}, from=1-2, to=1-3] \arrow["r", from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{s_g}}}"{description}, from=2-1, to=2-2] \arrow[""{name=1, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "g"', curve={height=30pt}, from=2-1, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{t_g}}}"{description}, from=2-2, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{p_f}}}"{description}, draw=none, from=0, to=1-2] \arrow["{{{p_g}}}"{description}, draw=none, from=1, to=2-2] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} Since $\mathsf{fact _{\L, \RI (r \circ f)$ is contractible, so is its identity type \begin{equation}\mleft(\im(f), s_f , r \circ t_f, \assoc(r, t_f, s_f) \cdot \ap_{r \circ {-}}(p_f) \mright) \ = \ \mleft(\im(g), s_g \circ l, t_g, \assoc(t_g, s_g, l)^{-1} \cdot \ap_{{-} \circ l}(p_g) \cdot S \mright)\end{equation} By \cref{fillid , the following type is also contractible: \begin{equation}\subalign{ \sum_{e : \im(f) \simeq_{\C} \im(g)}\sum_{H_{\L} : s_g \circ l = e \circ s_f} \quad \sum_{H_{\RI} : r \circ t_f = t_g \circ e} \ap_{t_g \circ {-}}(H_{\L}) \cdot \assoc(t_g, e, s_f)^{-1} \cdot \ap_{{-} \circ s_f}(H_{\RI})^{-1} \cdot \assoc(r, t_f, s_f) \cdot \ap_{r \circ {-}}(p_f) = \assoc(t_g, s_g, l)^{-1} \cdot \ap_{{-} \circ l}(p_g) \cdot S}\end{equation} By the bicategory structure of $\C$, we have the chain of equivalences shown on the next page.}}}

elements.sent-2411-647: Define \begin{equation}\mleft(\varphi, \alpha\mright) \ : \ \mleft(\bigvee_{x : \Delta}G_x\mright) \to \colimm_{x : \Gamma}\zeta(\Delta, G,x) \varphi(\inl(a)) \ \coloneqq \ \iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a) \varphi(\inr(i, x)) \ \coloneqq \ \iota_{\inl(i)}(x) \ap_{\varphi}(\glue(a,i)) \ = \ \kappa_{\zeta(\Delta,G)}(\inr(\ast), \inl(i),a )^{-1} \cdot \ap_{\iota_{\inl(i)}}(\pr_2(G_{\inr(\ast), \inl(i), \ast})(a)) \alpha \ \coloneqq \ \lambda{a}.\refl_{\iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a)}\end{equation} Conversely, define \begin{equation}\psi \ : \ \mleft(\colimm_{x : \Gamma}\zeta(\Delta, G,x) \mright) \to \bigvee_{x : \Delta}G_x \psi(\iota_{\inr(\ast)}(a)) \ \coloneqq \ \inl(a) \psi(\iota_{\inl(i)}(x)) \ \coloneqq \ \inr(i,x) \ap_{\psi}(\kappa_{\zeta(\Delta,G)}(\inr(\ast), \inl(i),a)) \ = \ \ap_{\inr(i, {-})}(\pr_2(G_{\inr(\ast), \inl(i), \ast})(a)) \cdot \glue(a, i)^{-1}\end{equation} It is easy to prove that $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are mutual inverses as ordinary functions.

elements.sent-2411-684: Note that for each $a : A$, \begin{equation}\tilde{\xi}_{i,j,g}(a) \ : \ \xi_{i,j,g}(\pr_2(F_i)(a))^{-1} \cdot \ap_{\pr_1(G_{i,j,g})}(\pr_2(d_i)(a)) \cdot \pr_2(G_{i,j,g})(a) = \ap_{\pr_1(d_j)}(\pr_2(F_{i,j,g})(a)) \cdot \pr_2(d_j)(a)\end{equation} We may assume that $\tilde{\xi _{i,j,g (a)$ instead has the equivalent type \begin{equation}\xi_{i,j,g}(\pr_2(F_i)(a)) = \underbrace{\ap_{\pr_1(G_{i,j,g})}(\pr_2(d_i)(a)) \cdot \pr_2(G_{i,j,g})(a) \cdot \pr_2(d_j)(a)^{-1} \cdot \ap_{\pr_1(d_j)}(\pr_2(F_{i,j,g})(a))^{-1}}_{E_{i,j,g}(a)}\end{equation} The triangle \begin{equation}\label{tripsi} \begin{tikzcd} {\colimm_{\Gamma}{A}} {\colimm_{\Gamma}(\F(F))} {\colimm_{\Gamma}(\F(G))} \arrow["{\psi_F}"', from=1-2, to=2-1] \arrow["{\psi_G}", from=1-2, to=2-3] \arrow["{\hat{\delta}}"', from=2-1, to=2-3] \end{tikzcd} \tag{$tri-$\psi\end{equation}}$$ commutes by induction on $\colimm_{\Gamma {A}$.}}}

elements.formula-2411-368: \begin{equation}w_1(y_1(\inl(i,j,g,x))) \ \equiv \ w_1(\iota_j(\pr_1(F_{i,j,g})(x))) \ \equiv \ \inr(j, \pr_1(F_{i,j,g})(x)) \ = \ \inl(i,j,g,x) \tag{$\glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1}$} w_1(y_1(\inr(i,x))) \ \equiv \ w_1(\iota_i(x)) \equiv \inr(i,x) \transport^{z \mapsto w_1(y_1(z)) = z}(\glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x)), \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1}) \quad = \ap_{w_1}(\ap_{y_1}(\glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x))))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x)) \quad = \ap_{w_1}(\kappa_{i,j,g}(x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x)) \quad = \mleft( \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x)) \mright)^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inl(i,j,g,x)) \quad = \refl_{\inr(i,x)} \transport^{z \mapsto w_1(y_1(z)) = z}(\glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x)), \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1}) \quad = \ap_{w_1}(\ap_{y_1}(\glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x)) \quad = \ap_{w_1}(\refl_{\iota_j(\pr_1(F_{i,j,g})(x))})^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x))^{-1} \cdot \glue_{H_1}(\inr(i,j,g,x)) \quad = \refl_{\inr(j,\pr_1(F_{i,j,g})(x))}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-717: The map \eqref{cofmap fits into an isomorphism of spans \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[column sep = large, row sep = huge] A [6mm] {\colimm_{\Gamma}{A}} {\colimm_{\Gamma}(\F(F))} {H_3} [6mm] {H_2} {H_1} \arrow["\psi", from=1-2, to=1-3] \arrow["{{{\mleft[\idd_A\mright]}}}"', from=1-2, to=1-1] \arrow["\simeq", from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow[""{name=0, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "{{{\inl}}}"', from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["\simeq", from=1-2, to=2-2] \arrow[""{name=1, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "{{{{{w_0}}}}}"', from=1-2, to=2-2] \arrow["\simeq"', from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{{{w_1}}}}}", from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{\eta_1}}}"', from=2-2, to=2-3] \arrow["{{{\eta_2}}}", from=2-2, to=2-1] \arrow["{\glue_{H_3}(a)^{-1}}"{description}, draw=none, from=0, to=1] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} which gives us an equivalence $\tau_0 : \colimm_{\Gamma ^A{F \xrightarrow{\simeq P_H$ of pushouts.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-719: We say that $\colimm_{\Gamma ^A(F)$ is universal, or pullback-stable, if for every pullback square \begin{equation}\label{pbs} \begin{tikzcd} {\colimm_{\Gamma}^A(F) \times_V Y} Y {\colimm_{\Gamma}^A(F)} V \arrow["{\pi_2}", from=1-1, to=1-2] \arrow["{\pi_1}"', from=1-1, to=2-1] \arrow["\lrcorner"{anchor=center, pos=0.125}, draw=none, from=1-1, to=2-2] \arrow["h", from=1-2, to=2-2] \arrow["f"', from=2-1, to=2-2] \end{tikzcd} \tag{$pb$}\end{equation} in $A/\U$, the canonical (cogap) map \begin{equation}\sigma_{f,h} \ : \ \colimm_{i : \Gamma}^A(F_i \times_V Y) \to_A \colimm_{\Gamma}^A(F) \times_V Y\end{equation} is an equivalence.\footnote{The $A$-cocone inducing this cogap map is actually nontrivial to construct and relies on the bicategorical structure of $A/\U$.}}
elements.sent-2411-720: See \cite[\href{https://github.com/PHart3/colimits-agda/blob/v0.3.0/Pullback-stability/Stability-cos-coc.agda {Stability-cos-coc ]{agda-colim-TR for a mechanized contruction of the map.}}}
elements.sent-2411-721: Consider the free functor $\mleft(({- ) + A\mright): \U \to A/\U$.}
elements.sent-2411-722: Let $\mleft(C, r, K\mright)$ be a limiting $A$-cone over $F$.
elements.sent-2411-723: For each $X : \U$, it's easy to check that the composite of equivalences \begin{equation}\ X \to \pr_1(C) \simeq \ \mleft(X + A\mright) \to_A C \simeq \ \limm_{i : \Gamma}(\mleft(X + A\mright) \to_A F_i) \simeq \ \limm_{i : \Gamma}(X \to \pr_1(F_i))\end{equation} equals the function $ \mleft(\F(C, r, K) \circ {-}\mright)$, which is thus an equivalence.

elements.sent-2411-753: The following type is contractible: \begin{equation}fact_{\widehat{\L},\widehat{\RI}}(h, \alpha) \ \coloneqq \ \sum_{A : \D_{\Gamma}}\sum_{S : F \Rightarrow A}\sum_{T : A \Rightarrow G}\mleft(T \circ S \sim \mleft(h, \alpha\mright)\mright) \times \widehat{\L}(S) \times \widehat{\RI}(T)\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-756: To this end, note that $\mathsf{fact _{\widehat{\L ,\widehat{\RI (h, \alpha)$ is equivalent to the type of data \begin{equation} A_0 \ : \ \Gamma_0 \to \U S_0 \ : \ \prod_{i : \Gamma_0}F_i \to A_i T_0 \ : \ \prod_{i : \Gamma_0}A_i \to G_i P \ : \ \prod_{i : \Gamma_0} T_i \circ S_i \sim h_i L \ : \ \prod_{i : \Gamma_0}\L(S_i) R \ : \ \prod_{i : \Gamma_0}\RI(T_i)  \qquad  A_1 \ : \ \prod_{i,j : \Gamma_0}\prod_{g : \Gamma_1(i,j)}A_0(i) \to A_0(j) S_1 \ : \ \prod_{i,j,g} A_{i,j,g} \circ S_i \sim S_j \circ F_{i,j,g} T_1 \ : \ \prod_{i,j,g} G_{i,j,g} \circ T_i \sim T_j \circ A_{i,j,g} p \ : \ \prod_{i,j,g}\prod_{x : F_i} \mleft(T_1 \ast S_1\mright)(i,j,g,x) = \ap_{G_{i,j,g}}(P_i(x)) \cdot \alpha_{i,j,g}(x) \cdot P_j(F_{i,j,g}(x))^{-1} \end{equation} We can contract the six left-hand fields because $\mathsf{fact _{\L, \RI (h_i)$ is contractible for each $i : \Gamma_0$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-821: We say that $L$ is 2-coherent if for all $h_1 : \homm_{\D (L(X), Y)$, $h_2 : \homm_{\C (Z, X)$, and $h_3 : \homm_{\C (W, Z)$, the following diagram commutes: \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd}[column sep = huge] {\mleft( \alpha(h_1) \circ h_2\mright) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1) \circ \mleft(h_2 \circ h_3\mright)} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2)) \circ h_3} {\alpha(h_1 \circ L(h_2 \circ h_3))} {\alpha(\mleft(h_1 \circ L(h_2)\mright) \circ L(h_3))} {\alpha(h_1 \circ \mleft(L(h_2) \circ L(h_3) \mright))} \arrow["{{\assoc(\alpha(h_1), h_2, h_3)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-1, to=1-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_2 \circ h_3, h_1)}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=1-3, to=2-3] \arrow["{{\ap_{{-} \circ h_3}(V_2(h_2,h_1))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-1, to=1-1] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\ap_{h_1 \circ {-}}(L_{\circ}(h_2,h_3)))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=2-3, to=3-3] \arrow["{{V_2(h_3,h_1 \circ L(h_2))}}", Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=2-1] \arrow["{{\ap_{\alpha}(\assoc(h_1, L(h_2), L(h_3)))}}"', Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=3-3] \end{tikzcd}\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-822: A cocone $\mleft(C, r, K\mright)$ under $F$ is colimiting if for all $X : \obb(\C)$, \begin{equation}\postcomp(C,r, K,X) \ : \ \homm_{\C}(C, X) \to \limm_{i : \Gamma^{\op}}(\homm_{\C}(F_i, X)) \postcomp(C,r,K,X,f) \ \coloneqq \ \mleft(\lambda{i}.f \circ r_i, \lambda{j}\lambda{i}\lambda{g}.\assoc(f, r_j, F_{i,j,g}) \cdot \ap_{f \circ {-}}(K_{i,j,g}) \mright)\end{equation} is an equivalence.
